lines.text: The gods are not present here. Their relics are. The distinction is considered important by those who understand it and irrelevant by those who do not.
Incense and the weight of belief. This level was built by people who thought the dungeon was holy — and by the standards they used for holiness, they were not entirely wrong.
The sacred vessel in the corner was used for purposes the surface world calls ritual and the dungeon calls accurate. The distinction is smaller than either side acknowledges.
The scriptures here have been revised across three theological traditions. Each revision preserved the previous version in the margins. The dungeon respects this methodology.
Something in the lower section of this level was worshipped by the civilization that built it. The thing that was worshipped is still present. The civilization is not.
Gold and bone — the materials of reverence in every tradition. The dungeon has both in abundance. It treats neither as more precious than the knowledge stored alongside them.
The scorch marks on the ceiling predate the dungeon's construction. Whatever burned there burned before the first stone was laid. The dungeon built around the damage.
A ritual was performed in this room at regular intervals for approximately four hundred years. The performance wore grooves in the stone — the worshippers' paths, preserved like fossils.
What the gods knew, the dungeon knows. What the dungeon knows about the gods is sealed in the lowest section of this level. The interpretation differs from the scriptures significantly.
The god's reliquary holds what remains of beings that were either divine or believed to be — which, the dungeon notes, produces identical archaeological evidence.
